skill.name: tdd
skill.description: Guides test-driven development using a red-green-refactor loop, behavior-focused tests, interface design, mocking boundaries, and refactoring discipline.
skill.summary: Apply TDD through one behavior at a time, from failing test to minimal implementation and refactor.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, network, and environment access patterns. Review found Markdown code fences, diagrams, prose, and illustrative TypeScript examples rather than executable skill behavior or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Findings Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts point to Markdown code fences and examples, not executable commands. No command invocation instruction or shell payload was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown code blocks or instructional examples. There is no script file, command runner, or instruction to execute shell code.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Access Are Instructional Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The fetch calls and STRIPE_KEY reference appear inside TypeScript examples about mockable interfaces. They are not active skill code and do not exfiltrate secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are fenced TypeScript snippets used to explain dependency injection and API wrappers. No runtime path sends environment values to a network endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Labels Are Heuristic Noise
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts map to prose, headings, diagrams, and testing advice. No cryptographic API, host discovery, or system enumeration behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found educational text and examples only. The flagged files contain no cryptographic functions, scanner commands, or host information collection.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The critical combination alert depends on lower-level false positives. No file combines code execution, credential access, and network transfer in executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only credential and network patterns are examples, and the execution pattern is Markdown formatting. The semantic combination required for exfiltration is absent.

content.value_statement: Teams often write tests after implementation, which can miss behavior and lock in fragile designs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused red-green-refactor cycles.
actual_capabilities: Plans behavior-first TDD work with user confirmation before coding.
Guides one failing test and one minimal implementation at a time.
Encourages tests through public interfaces instead of private internals.
Explains when to mock external APIs, databases, time, randomness, and file systems.
Supports interface design choices that make tests simpler and more stable.
Identifies refactor candidates after tests pass.
limitations: It does not provide a runnable test framework or automation script.
It does not choose exact edge cases without project context.
It assumes the assistant can inspect and edit the target codebase.
It gives general testing guidance, not language-specific framework documentation.

use_cases.title: Add a Feature Safely
use_cases.description: Use a failing behavior test before implementing a new public capability.

use_cases.title: Fix a Regression
use_cases.description: Capture the broken behavior first, then make the smallest change that restores it.

use_cases.title: Improve Test Design
use_cases.description: Replace implementation-coupled tests with behavior-focused tests that survive refactors.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use TDD to add this feature. First identify the public behavior, then write one failing test before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Bug Test First
prompt_templates.scenario: Bug fix with unknown root cause.
prompt_templates.prompt: Reproduce this bug with one behavior-focused test. After it fails, make the minimal fix and run the relevant tests.

prompt_templates.title: Review Test Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these tests for implementation coupling. Suggest behavior-focused replacements and identify which mocks should remain at system boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Incremental Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the existing tests as a safety net. Plan small refactor steps, run tests after each step, and keep public behavior unchanged.

output_examples.output: A short TDD plan, one behavior to test first, the expected failing result, and the minimal implementation path.

output_examples.input: These checkout tests fail after a refactor.
output_examples.output: A diagnosis of implementation coupling, replacement behavior checks, and mocking changes limited to external boundaries.

output_examples.input: Refactor this service after tests pass.
output_examples.output: A prioritized refactor checklist with test checkpoints after each small design change.

best_practices: Confirm the public interface and most important behaviors before writing tests.
Write one failing test, implement only enough code, then repeat.
Mock system boundaries, but test internal behavior through public APIs.
anti_patterns: Writing all tests before any implementation feedback exists.
Testing private methods, call counts, or internal collaborators by default.
Refactoring while tests are still failing.

faq.question: Does this skill write code automatically?
faq.answer: It guides the assistant through TDD decisions. Code changes depend on the user request and available project files.

faq.question: Can it work with any test framework?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is framework-neutral and focuses on behavior, public interfaces, and incremental verification.

faq.question: When should mocks be used?
faq.answer: Use mocks for system boundaries such as external APIs, time, randomness, file systems, or some databases.

faq.question: Does it recommend integration tests only?
faq.answer: It favors integration-style tests through public interfaces, but it does not ban smaller tests when they verify behavior.

faq.question: How does it handle refactoring?
faq.answer: It waits until tests pass, then suggests small refactor steps with test runs after each step.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found instructional examples only, with no confirmed malicious behavior or executable dangerous pattern.
